title: How to be part of the Cultural Heritage Cloud?
content:
ECHOES has published its booklet How to be part of the Cultural Heritage Cloud? 

The booklet presents the context in which the ECHOES project proposes its Cloud Architecture, its Data Model and its Integration Framework and explains how institutions, projects and stakeholders can take part in building the Cultural Heritage Cloud by integrating their resources into this emerging European Digital Commons.

The framework draws on the ECHOES project deliverables D3.1 Integration Strategy, D3.2 Integration Roadmap, D6.1 Data Strategy for the Cultural Heritage Knowledge Base, and D6.2 Interoperability Guidelines.

View and download the booklet from Zenodo.
snippet: ECHOES has published its booklet How to be part of the Cultural Heritage Cloud?
creator: Sheena_B
sourceDescription: European Cloud for Heritage OpEn Science

title: ECHOLOT participates in the AI-Bridges Symposium
content:
Several members from ECHOLOT took part in the AI-BRIDGES symposium which took place at the Senate House, University of London, on the 29th May.

Lozana Rossenova speaking at the Expert Panel

Read the full article on the ECHLOT website.

The event, which took place under the theme ‘Bridging Institutions, Open Knowledge, and AI’, brought together a wide range of stakeholders, including Wikimedia chapters and affiliates, researchers, academics, technologists, open knowledge advocates, policymakers and funders. It focused on the urgent need to address the threats and opportunities posed by the ever-increasing use of AI methods in open knowledge spaces around the world.

ECHOLOT project coordinator, Lozana Rossenova from TIB, joined an expert panel to address the most urgent topics relevant to knowledge organisation on the web and AI applications. ECHOLOT’s approach was presented as one of the practical routes to increase collaboration across Wikimedia and EU academic communities through shared workflows that employ AI in a supportive, rather than extractive function. Other partners were involved in round tables during the Symposium.
snippet:
Several members from ECHOLOT took part in the AI-BRIDGES symposium which took place at the Senate House, University of London, on the 29th May.

Lozana Rossenova speaking at the Expert Panel
creator: Sheena_B
sourceDescription: European Cloud for Heritage OpEn Science

title: EXCALIBUR Chronicles Ep. 1: Project Meeting at the Tomb of the Egyptian in South Sardinia
content:
In this first episode, we visit Sant’Antioco in South Sardinia to explore the Punic necropolis and the fascinating Tomb of the Egyptian, one of EXCALIBUR’s four Pilot Sites. The video was filmed during the project’s two-day pilot visit and consortium meeting in Sardinia, bringing together archaeology, digital innovation, and burial heritage preservation.

Through this video, EXCALIBUR highlights how cutting-edge technologies can support the preservation, restoration, and presentation of funerary heritage, while making these sites more accessible and engaging for wider audiences.
snippet: In this first episode, we visit Sant’Antioco in South Sardinia to explore the Punic necropolis and the fascinating Tomb of the Egyptian, one of EXCALIBUR’s four Pilot Sites. The video was filmed

title: Digital Heritage Summit 2026
content:
Xavier Rodier will be taking part as a speaker at the Euromed/Digital Heritage Summit which takes place in Limassol, Cyprus from the 25th-29th May.  In addition to ECHOES, sister projects AUTOMATA and HERITALISE are also featuring as part of the programme.

Visit the Conference website for further information.

Organised by the UNESCO Chair on Digital Cultural Heritage at the Cyprus University of Technology, in partnership with Heritage Malta, the Digital Heritage Summit 2026 is this year’s special edition of EuroMed 2026 — one of Europe’s leading forums for digital cultural heritage research, policy, and innovation.

Bringing together world-class experts, institutions, and industry leaders, the Summit focuses on the transformational impact of digital technologies on culture, heritage, and society.

The Summit is in cooperation with Europeana, the European Commission (DG CONNECT), the AI4LAM initiative, the EUreka3D Competence Center, the Digital-Europe project EUreka3D-XR, the Horizon Europe projects HERITALISE, STECCI, IMPULSE, the Time Machine Organisation and more.

title: First Cascading Call selected projects announced
content:
ECHOES is thrilled to introduce the 12 projects that have been awarded funding by the first Cascading Call for contributions of data to the Cultural Heritage Cloud.

The Castel Sant’Angelo (MOH)

The Water Castle, Ruppendorf, Germany (RuDIHeritage)

The data being provided comes from:

  • FinFAIR: a Baltic–Nordic corpus with a workflow for creating Heritage Digital Twins,
  • Moher: Central European mosaic artworks with analytical and conservation data,
  • LandArchIE: landscape LiDAR and satellite archeological data from Ireland for prehistoric to early medieval time periods,
  • APACH: use cases based on an interoperable HBIM (Historic Building Information Modelling) framework,
  • M2M: 3D reconstructions of key monuments from Ephesus, Turkey,
  • SMARTWAYS: a cultural heritage Points of Interest (POIs) dataset from the Romea Strata and Via Francigena pilgrimage routes in Italy,
  • MOH: documentation and datasets from the Mausoleum of Hadrian, the Castel Sant’Angelo
  • SAVOR: a bilingual (Romanian–Italian) corpus of approximately 3,300 traditional recipes,
  • SPARRK-UA: datasets for at-risk cultural heritage in museum emergency settings in Ukraine,
snippet:
ECHOES is thrilled to introduce the 12 projects that have been awarded funding by the first Cascading Call for contributions of data to the Cultural Heritage Cloud.

The Castel Sant’Angelo (MOH)
creator: Sheena_B
sourceDescription: European Cloud for Heritage OpEn Science

title: Call for Papers: 23rd EUROGRAPHICS Workshop on Graphics and Cultural Heritage (GCH 2026)
content:
GCH 2026 has opened its Call for Papers which includes a special track on the ECCCH, “Visual Computing in the Context of the European Collaborative Cloud for Cultural Heritage”.

We are pleased to invite you to contribute to the 23rd EUROGRAPHICS Workshop on Graphics and Cultural Heritage (GCH 2026).

GCH 2026 (https://gch2026.virvig.eu/) serves as a premier forum for experts from both the ICT and Cultural Heritage (CH) sectors. The workshop aims to showcase how computer graphics, AI, and digital technologies are revolutionising the research, preservation, and dissemination of cultural heritage. We particularly encourage interdisciplinary submissions that foster collaboration between researchers, heritage professionals, and creative industries.

Date: This year’s event will be held from November 2nd to November 5th, 2026.
Location & Venue:  The workshop is hosted by the Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya (UPC) in the vibrant city of Barcelona, Spain.
Organisation: GCH 2026 is organised by the ViRVIG Research Centre (Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya) and supported by Eurographics.
snippet: GCH 2026 has opened its Call for Papers which includes a special track on the ECCCH, “Visual Computing in the Context of the European Collaborative Cloud for Cultural Heritage”.
creator: Sheena_B
sourceDescription: European Cloud for Heritage OpEn Science

title: A Day with TEXTaiLES with Hackathon-like event
content:
TextAIles are organising a one-day public event on the 3rd June as part of Oulu Cultural Capital 2026, Finland, as the University of Oulu is a member of their consortium. Open to everyone, from researchers and heritage professionals to students and curious members of the public, this is a unique opportunity to explore how cutting-edge artificial intelligence and digital tools are revolutionising the way we study, preserve, and understand textile cultural heritage.

The day is structured in two complementary sessions. The morning is dedicated to presentations and knowledge-sharing, giving attendees a thorough grounding in the TEXTaiLES project, its tools, and its place within the broader European cultural heritage ecosystem. The afternoon shifts into an interactive mode, offering both a hands-on hackathon and an open space for creative engagement.

No prior technical knowledge required, just a curiosity about textiles, heritage, and the technologies that are bringing them into the digital age.

Who Is This Event For?

title: MusicSphere: User Needs and Requirements Survey
content:
MusicSphere is currently in the phase of identifying user requirements and analysing the needs of the community. At this stage of the project, our objective is to better understand the expectations, challenges, and real practices of professionals working with cultural, musical and sound heritage of historic pipe organs.

To achieve this, we are engaging with a range of key stakeholders, including:

  • musical instrument makers and organ builders
  • luthiers
  • restoration and conservation professionals
  • academics and researchers
  • technology providers.

    • Their input is essential to ensure that the tools and solutions developed within MusicSphere are not only technically sound, but also relevant, usable, and aligned with real-world needs.

    This questionnaire is part of this process. It focuses on gathering insights related to current workflows, existing challenges, and expectations regarding tools for digitisation, analysis, and preservation of this type of heritage.

    By contributing to this phase, participants play an active role in shaping the development of user-oriented solutions, helping to bridge the gap between technological innovation and the practical realities of the cultural heritage sector.
snippet: MusicSphere is currently in the phase of identifying user requirements and analysing the needs of the community. At this stage of the project, our objective is to better understand the expectations,
creator: Sheena_B

title: COLOURS Workshop: Shaping the future of digital practices in Conservation-Restoration
content:
What if you could virtually test a restoration strategy before touching a single artwork? On the 12th May 2026, COLOURS is bringing this possibility — and much more — to conservators-restorers gathered in Ljubljana for a hands-on workshop attached to the General Assembly of E.C.C.O.

Taking place at the Archives of the Republic of Slovenia (Poljanska street 40), the workshop will introduce participants to the COLOURS-ECCCH Community of Practice and its suite of digital tools designed to bridge the gap between conservation-restoration expertise and digital efficiency.

Attendees will have the opportunity to explore and interact directly with the COLOURS tools, including advanced visualisation capabilities that overlay scientific data and condition maps onto high-fidelity 2D/3D models, predictive simulation tools to virtually test restoration strategies before physical intervention, and FAIR documentation systems connected to a cloud-based repository for seamless knowledge sharing.
snippet: What if you could virtually test a restoration strategy before touching a single artwork? On the 12th May 2026, COLOURS is bringing this possibility — and much more — to conservators-restorers
creator: Sheena_B
sourceDescription: European Cloud for Heritage OpEn Science

title: ECHOLOT Kick-off and Consortium Meetings
content:
Although the ECHOLOT project has only been operational for a few months, it has already held two plenary meetings, demonstrating strong engagement and enthusiasm for shaping the future of cultural heritage data in Europe.

ECHOLOT Consortium members

The official kick-off meeting of the ECHOLOT project took place online on the 14th and 15th January 2026. It provided an excellent opportunity to review all work packages, allocate tasks, and define the project’s roadmap for the next 36 months.

The second consortium meeting was held at the University of the Basque Country in Bilbao, Spain, from the 9th to 11th March. In addition to enabling partners to meet in person, the meeting focused on gathering requirements and coordinating activities. Special attention was given to collecting input from third-party participants involved in the case studies. A dedicated workshop was organised, featuring user-centred design activities such as co-designing user personas, user stories, and journey maps.
